item.content:encoded: Jeez. What a way to tell an engaging story without telling anything. Great work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This gave me the feeling of watching those long videos about exorcism and gave the same unnerving feeling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Allow me to sum up how I feel about this particular article.

Imagine me reading a book. Now, the introduction and rising action of the book are fantastic, so I keep on reading.

And then I realize that the pages in the book have been ripped out just as it was getting to the good part.

I really like the concept, but man, my rage at the fact that the mystery will remain unsolved makes me want to tear my hair out. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My eyes glazed over so many times while reading this. There was so little of interest until the last bit that I had to force myself to read through the constant expungements that not only failed to catch my interest at what they might be hiding, but actively made me lose what little investment I had. The thing is that the main idea is fine, but the constant annoyance of being taken out of the reading because of the jarring [DATA EXPUNGED] that pop every other line made this a chore to read.

-1, I still find articles that rely on heavy redactions or data expungmenets to be lazy.

Edit: Lazy is too harsh a word, but I still feel overusing redactions makes for a dull article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Best 1st April page I have ever seen

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope, you're definitely not alone here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one who's noticed a similarity to SCP-579 (in that a whole lot has been expunged and stuff)? It's even got "579" in the number.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably my favorite 5000 contest entry. You captured an intriguing premise and expanded on it without being overly convoluted, while simultaneously excluding so much information. I think that's the thing with a lot of your articles, what with managing to tow the line between Escher-esque amusement and esoteric confusion. It's a skill which makes me really respect you as an author. Good job with this one, and keep at it!

item.title: [RATING EXPUNGED]
item.content:encoded:
[MILESTONE REDACTED]

[CELEBRATION PURGED]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the writer’s idea of making the article mysterious, but the writer shouldn’t delete that much. It is the writer’s responsibility to make the readers understand what is going on. The writer at least should write something in the description part instead of just leaving it blank.
Think about it this way: if you expunged a data, it cannot be seen by anyone anymore. How do you suppose the MTFs to capture and destroy an object that they don’t even know what it looks like???
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I so so so so so so want to love this, it has all of the bones and almost enough meat and I may come back and upvote after I think about it for a while and feel regret for not doing so now, but, for right now, I just can't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
o

ok

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: what is it tho

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This just screams "MYSTERY" to me more than any other 5000 entry. It strikes juuuuuuuuuust the right balance in informing you and keeping you ignorant. +1, sad it won't win.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Technically speaking, even SCP-579 has more actual information on the subject itself than this: from SCP-579, it can be deduced that SCP-579 is an object, it can be contained within a given space, it can be suspended using electromagnets, it has a specific point of origin, etc. With this, one doesn't even know if it is an actual THING, or just some phenomena.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As per its official policy, the Foundation remains opposed to the use of non-human afterlives for personal or organisational gains.

SCP-3560 laughing in the distance

In all seriousness, I really liked this article. While I can understand this not grooving with others (that's a lot of DATA EXPUNGED my dude), as a mystery I'm thoroughly intrigued. I want to know more about why the Foundation has gone to these lengths to wipe this from virtually all memory. Why is it so dangerous? What happened here?

And yeah, it's frustrating for the mystery to still be up in the air by the time the article concludes, but there is no requirement here that the mystery needs to be solved by the end of the article, and it was a very enjoyable ride up to that point.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
mediums are just a thing the Foundation believes in and uses for containment now

From the article:

The use of Protocol ICON as standard info-containment for infohazardous objects is under consideration, pending the development of a method of expanding the scope of SCP-5000's secondary properties

Emphasis mine. Nothing about this presupposes any religious or occult beliefs are ordinarily valid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As an experimental document, it's pretty solid, exciting even. We've had procedures expunged, we've had description expunged, porque no los dos?

But as an article, it's quite unsatisfying for reasons others have already pointed out. The two things I'd point to are the whole "don't try to extrapolate what this is from the above document", because seriously, we can't, plus the whole "mediums are just a thing the Foundation believes in and uses for containment now" idea. Those are always a hard sell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is religious iconography, but it's also been realised. The word choice here was careful and deliberate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A quite decent example of a 579-type; doesn't do anything especially groundbreaking with this little subgenre but was executed solidly and entertainingly. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
to be brutally honest, I'm not crazy about this one. it feels like 579, 3125, and cousin johnny (3004 and the other number I can't recall at the moment) got all smushed together, culminating in this piece.

there's also far too much mystery here and not enough substance. I understand that mystery is the theme, but there's so much of it here that what little information is in there just isn't really compelling. there's no content here that makes me so invested in the mystery that I daydream about what's in the gaps.

it's a novote for me.

also: "Any realised iconography is to be extracted from the cerebellum and incinerated."

do you mean religious iconography?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love Information is contained in the ghost.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i figured someone would see the 5000 contest theme of "mystery" and decide to do that SCP-055 / SCP-579 thing where everything is [DATA EXPUNGED]. the addition of actual images and weird elements (the mention of "cerebrospinal fluid and holy water" was pretty neat) is not enough for this to be yet another slog of "expungment as substitute for horror".

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This trick has been done before, and there honestly wasn't even enough here to pique my curiosity and leave me wanting to know more. It' just "something vaguely religious and you can't talk about it." That said, I really like the idea of an "informational afterlife" and wish that had been more of the article's focus. I'm downvoting for now, may or may not change to a no-vote later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels a lot like SCP-579, though it does differ quite a bit in specific places. I think it still works, it is just not for me.

item.title: Site Criticism Team - Staff Review
item.content:encoded:
The above sections cannot and should not be used to attempt to discern the nature of SCP-5000.

This line right here encapsulates the problems I have with this article. Us as readers have no way at all of knowing what this is, what it does, nothing. This takes the classic horror trope of "the monster is always scarier in the viewer's mind" and dials it up to 100, and it doesn't work for me.

In a way, this almost feels like a rehash of SCP-4107, only you've swapped the intrigue and inherent mystery of that article, of knowing that there is an answer and that it is possible to figure out, with the disappointment of knowing that there is an answer and it isn't possible to figure out. It's an Easter egg we never get to open.

The above is why I've downvoted.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to TheMightyMcB or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Interpretation 
- Interpretation

That picture looks like a cicada.

item.title: The site's foremost conceptual author delivers a bang.
item.content:encoded:
Kudos for tackling some pretty big taboos with extreme precision and tact. I especially appreciate the article's reliance on the containment procedures alone to convey the idea.

I think my only problem with this is located at, if not near, the very first step from the contest's premise: that the SCP itself is going to be the mystery here. I feel like this was the initial divide when thinking about an entry, and while this may be the best (only?) of those articles that came down on that side of it, I think it is the lower hanging fruit of an entry from the get-go. That is not to say it is easier to pull off. I think you've done about as good of a job as I think can be done.

A bit of trivia; this article culminates two gradual trends of the wiki's subject matter over time: towards more concept-based SCPs and an increase in the number of articles that include religion/ritual.

Ultimately I'm glad we have contests like this that can uniquely justify an article that behaves this way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I lovehate this piece. The amount missing intrigues and infuriates me. What you've included almost traces the shape around the informational hole, but not quite. I got instant SCP-579 vibes and it feels very much like a modern attempt to do the same… probably because that's exactly what it is.

Infohazards are such a fuck. Super enthralling and completely unsatisfying.

The shit of it is, I know exactly the kind of hazard this would be necessary for…

Dammit.

+1. Begrudgingly. Somehow it works or I wouldn't still be thinking about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very mixed feelings. On the one hand, lines like "actualized occultism to release the spirits of incinerated documents" and "any realised iconography is to be extracted from the cerebellum and incinerated" are absolutely brilliant, and the writing overall is really, really good. On the other hand, despite the high quality of the content present, the amount missing does make it hard for me to get really invested in the piece. Torn between upvote and novote, so probably leaving as novote for the moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. So, correct me if I'm mistaken, but

+ show block
– hide block

is this an expunged religion? With perhaps Christianity taking its place for whatever reason? Maybe I'm missing something, but a tiny, tiny little hint of the "why" would go a long way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: nice details, and I like the idea, but its kinda blah

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooh, I remember seeing this in chat! Quite mysterious. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So this is a great big iconoclasm? Other than that, there just isn't any information and it feels more like a wall with [DATA EXPUNGED] painted over it than a mystery. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you see the article's rating swinging up and down by a single vote for hours on end, that's probably me, obsessively no- and upvoting as I grapple with my feelings about the article. The concept is certainly really cool and had me grinning at points; the issue is that, yeah, it does feel like there needs to be more meat on the bones regarding the actual anomaly, lest this fall into 579's niche. It's brilliant worldbuilding, but I don't think it's more than that.

Hence the novote/upvote swing, tending more to novote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll be honest. This feels way too close to SCP-579. There are some differences in the specifics, but it doesn't feel different enough to me to warrant an upvote. Maybe I missed something though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bold move to expunge what the damn thing is, but you make it work. I feel like the smart use of redaction is one of the most important things about the writing on this site, and you put a new spin on it in the sense that this NEEDS to be redacted. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is the most torn i've been on an entry so far. on one hand this tells us just barely enough to go off of (which i suppose is the hook) and it tells a somewhat satisfying story. i cant say i walked away feeling wow'ed, but it was satisfying enough. im unsure as to how i would feel about it as the 5000, but this is a good article anyway so i will upvote it. well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty solid. I was pretty amused by the boldness in writing an article with the class, procedures and description with the infamous EXPUNGED and making it work. I have my reservations - there isn't a lot to go on here, as others have said, and I admit my feelings are more of 'ok dude, you got me, you did it' than 'wow what a great and compelling story', but hell, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
absolutely a good job for the "mystery", but may not suitable for 5k.

maybe +1 after the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this fucking owns, i love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a really entertaining read, and I feel like you were able to pull off the shtick of the article perfectly. I can look past all the data expunges, because it fits with the article so it doesn't become jarring at any point. The premise and execution of said premise make up for the lack of any concrete information on the actual anomaly itself, +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+Opinion
– hide block

I'm a bit conflicted on this one. On one hand, this entry satisfies the contest theme nigh-perfectly, giving the reader just enough clues to start formulating their own theories about the anomaly, and the implication of the actual anomaly itself being some sort of infohazardous religion that the Foundation banished to the informational afterlife, combined with some absolutely fantastic lines throughout the article like "Any realised iconography is to be extracted from the cerebellum and incinerated" and "Subtle short-term procedures are also planned, [DATA EXPUNGED] increase biological similarity between cerebrospinal fluid and holy water to lower death toll", had me finishing the article feeling extremely intrigued and curious. On the other hand… I feel like this is an instance of leaving too much up to the reader, and having the article suffer for that. You present us with this extremely interesting premise, but the lack of any definitive clues ultimately makes the creation of my own interpretations feel unsatisfying, since I don't really have much to base my interpretations off of. Even just one hint towards the actual nature of the anomaly would've been enough to push me towards an upvote, but given the nature of the anomaly itself, I understand if that's not really possible. In the end, I guess it just comes down to personal preference, and what each individual reader necessarily wants out of the idea of a "mystery". No-vote, but I may change my mind after further consideration or after the contest is over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
where were you when data was kill

I was at house drinking diet ghost

wiki updated

"data is kill"

"no"

item.title: Junior Staff - Site Crit
item.content:encoded:
[+ Opinion]
– hide block

I never thought I'd even give the time of day to anything with this many crucial sections expunged. Then Pedantique's proposal happened. Then this happened. Both have made me go against this rule, and boy am I happy that I did.

I'll mention that I entirely understand some of the more negative feedback on this, but I feel my opinion on this closely echos Nat's. This is brief and raises a great amount of questions, while giving us enough information to start formulating our own explanations. For once, I like that there's no spoilers here because I don't want to be told my interpretation is wrong.

You raise the intensity and absurdity extremely well with lines like "Any realised iconography is to be extracted from the cerebellum and incinerated." The image of the Foundation removing some information from a brain and physically destroying it is just something I absolutely adore for some reason.

So overall? I understand why this is controversial and is receiving some downvotes, but for what you're going for I feel this is near-perfect. To change it too much would damage its charm, I feel.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to gee0765 or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: X000 entries aren't *supposed* to be showstoppers. Most people write them that way because it grabs attention, yes, but no requirement for it exists. You don't have to write an X000 that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Largely agree. Resurrecting information and the concept of an information-afterlife are compelling, but a lot of this just reads like a dangerous expunged infohazard type deal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually quite like this, and am going to upvote it on that grounds, but I think it'd make a terrible 5000 simply because those are (generally agreed to be) supposed to be big, showstopper articles, whereas this is rather thinky and…idk, cute?

Also props for using the word 'necromancy' in the classical sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I seriously loved this in the draft phase and I still do. Informational seances are one hell of a clever concept, and making it be for what seems to be a Foundation-erased religion lends a lot of creepiness to it. This is the type of clever, informational anomaly I love seeing on this wiki.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with you. The theme fits in very well, but it totally destroys the narrative. It's a thing that we must not know about and it does something. It should be more like a thing that does a thing that we have no idea how it works/how to handle it, with a little unique narrative to it. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’ve read this three times now and I still dislike the description being expunged. I know that’s the idea, but meta SCPs are extremely hard to do right and I don’t think this deserves the 5000 spot.

It’s well written and a good idea but this is entirely based on my overall enjoyment of the article. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree, there just isn't enough here for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of an infohazardous anomaly having to be purged from the world is really interesting, and all the protocols outlined don't disappoint, but this really didn't do much for me. I got to the end and it didn't really feel like all that satisfying of a conclusion— I wanted to know more. I get that the theme of the contest is mystery— and there's definitely one there— but following the theme shouldn't undercut the narrative.

Novote for now, I personally think this needs a bit more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I never thought that I'd consider, even for a moment, to upvote an article wherein the object class, containment procedures and description all have a big fat [DATA EXPUNGED] plastered over them.

Still, I considered, and came to the conclusion that this article is worthy of a firm no-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a number of minor nitpicky problems with this that I could have otherwise overlooked if the article as a whole had worked for me, but unfortunately it didn't. Ultimately, I just find mysteries that remain completely unresolved like this to be very unsatisfying. The format of the article is interesting, but ultimately comes across as justification for not actually explaining anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Data is kill

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
This was my entry into the SCP-5000 contest. The theme was mystery.

Spoilers: [REDACTED]

Image 1: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Mikhail_Vrubel_-_Flying_Demon_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg
Image 2: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Seance_Doll.jpg
